title: Hermann Staratzkys Kriegstagebuch "Der Große Krieg" | War diary of Hermann Staratzky "The Great War"
classification_reason: Scores are close, so this segment should be treated as a manual-review candidate.
text: The author, then 17 years old, reports in detail in his diary how he experienced the time immediately before and after the outbreak of war in 1914. He writes about the mood at the time of the impending entry into the war and in the first days of the war, the mood among travelers, in the city, among peers, among his teachers, at the barracks gate, with his mother and also with himself the author has spent a Wandervogel holiday in the Eifel and is now alone on a hike along the Lahn. But soon he drives back to his hometown of Hamburg and reports from there. The records cover the period from July 26 to August 24, 1914. Hermann does not survive the war. He is wounded and dies on October 22, 1916.

title: FRAD067-123/2 Second album de Nicolas Hiessler (1884-1952), infirmier en Flandre et en Artois (armée allemande) | Album from Nicolas Hiessler, with photographs from the Western Front
classification_reason: Scores are close, so this segment should be treated as a manual-review candidate.
text: It is in memory of Miss Valentine Hiessler (born in Oberbronn on May 31, 1914, deceased), that she was still going to visit in 2006 at the retirement home of Haguenau, which Mrs. Teresa Weller entrusted to the Departmental Archives of Bas- Rhine two photographic albums from the father of this acquaintance, Constable Nicolas Hiessler (31/10/1884-13/05/1952). Consultation of this man's individual Veteran's file (Arch. dep. Bas-Rhin, call number 844 D 165) makes it possible to retrace his career and corroborate dates and places captioning his war photographs. Private, non-commissioned officer (03/28/1915), then nurse-sergeant (01/20/1918), Nicolas Hiessler served from August 3, 1914 to November 22, 1918, essentially (from October 31, 1914) in the Bayerische Sanitäts Kraftwagen Abteilung 6. As such he took part in position combat in Flanders and Artois from October 14, 1915 to June 23, 1916 and from January 1, 1917 to April 8, 1918 - services which he still in 1946, difficulties in asserting as justifying his rights to retirement as a combatant. He also took part in the fighting to defend the border in Lorraine (08-19/08/1914, within the 42. inf. Div. (XXI AK)), in the battles near La Bassée, Arras (09/05 -23/07 and 25/09-13/10/1915) and Armentières (09-18/04/1918); he ended the war on the western front, in Flanders and still in Artois (19/04-06/09/1918). Holder of the “Mil. Fuhrerschein 3 b”, Nicolas Hiessler was decorated twice: Bay. mil. Green. Kreuz 3. Kl. md Kr. u. Schw. (27/11/1916); Prof. Eis. Kreuz 2. Klasse (07/10/1918). The two photographic albums he kept from the war attest to his passage, between 1916 and 1918, in Brussels, Valenciennes, Anzin, Arras, Hénin-Liétard (today Hénin-Beaumont), Croizilles, Hunes, Winkles, Loos-en -Gohelle, Lens, Loison-sous-Lens, Pont-à-Vendin, Vimy, Douchy-les-Mines, Mouchy, Roubaix, Ostend, Tournai, Bailleul, Herlies, Raismes… See also contribution FRAD067-123/1.

title: Wilhelm Sandvoß´ Einsatz an der Ost- und Westfront | Wilhelm Sandvoß mission at Eastern and Western Front
classification_reason: Scores are close, so this segment should be treated as a manual-review candidate.
text: Wilhelm Sandvoß came from Segeste in the district of Alfeld. During the war he served in the 10th company of the Reserve Infantry Regiment 249, which was deployed on the Eastern Front and in the last year of the war also on the Western Front. The surviving photos testify to his participation in the Battle of Kovel, trench warfare between Krevo-Smorgon-Noroch-Tverech and the Battle of Riga. In his letter to his relatives in February 1916, he reports on the everyday hardships, for example with regard to food or life in the trenches. The last photo dates from May 1918 in Montdidier on the Somme. Nothing else is known about his life story after the war.

title: FRAD066_091 Georges Dubos et Marcel Vincent | Documents of Georges Dubos and Marcel Vincent
classification_reason: Scores are close, so this segment should be treated as a manual-review candidate.
text: Georges Dubos is from Gironde. An orphan, he was raised by his great-aunt. He studied violin at the Bordeaux Conservatory. Belonging to the 1917 class, he benefited from a reprieve for health reasons until 1918. On the front (Seine-et-Marne), a comrade made him a violin from a cigar box. We see him playing in the photographs, he also mentions it in his war diary During the war, he met Marcel Vincent: they became friends, so much so that Georges Dubos went on leave with his comrade in the family of this last. There he met his sister Yvonne: they married in 1925, the time Georges made a "situation". On his return from the war, he gave up the violin, if not for his leisure, and entered the administration. Marcel Vincent also survived the war; he was gassed which caused him emphysema.

title: Lettre du Commandant Ménétrier | Letter about Commandant Ménétrier
classification_reason: Scores are close, so this segment should be treated as a manual-review candidate.
text: Major Louis Menetrier was squadron leader in the 61st artillery regiment. An officer of great value, he was killed in action on September 8, 1915. A letter sent four months after his death evokes a reconnaissance ride on horseback in the springtime and seemingly unreal atmosphere of this war, and the health of his men and the 500 horses for which he was responsible. He was cited twice in the orders of the Army and was made a member of the Legion of Honor.

title: FRBNBU-108 Un suédois au service de la France | Knut William Mattson a physiotherapist in France service

text: Story told by Germaine Mattson, daughter-in-law of Knut William Mattson (medical doctor in Sweden whose diploma had not been recognized in France). Although not mobilized, being Swedish and married to a Franco-Swedish woman and living in Paris, he had deemed it his duty to make himself available to the country at war which had welcomed him. He therefore offered his services to the Red Cross as a masseur for the wounded from August 2, 1914 to November 11, 1918, which entitled him to the commemorative medal of the great war for the good care given to the wounded in CRF ambulances and in the ambulances of the Rothschild family.

title: FRAD087_062-"Notes de voyage" de Martial Daigueperse | Diary and documents from Martial Daigueperse
classification_reason: Scores are close, so this segment should be treated as a manual-review candidate.
text: Story of Martial Daigueperse told by his granddaughter. Martial Daigueperse was the only son of a coachman living in Saint-Germain-les-Belles (Haute-Vienne) and a servant born in Bujaleuf (Haute-Vienne). He learned the profession of typographer and worked in Limoges at the Bontemps printing works, specializing in scientific works. He worked there while on leave. During the war, he was a gunner serving in the 34th Artillery Regiment. The war saw him pass through Champagne, and he fought in particular at Verdun, without being injured. Martial Daigueperse kept a diary, written in pencil on two notebooks, which he called "Notes de voyage". This diary covers the period 1914-1919, from his departure from Limoges until his return from the front. He observes daily life at the front, adopting a very neutral tone. He describes the construction of the shelters, the cooking he often does for the group to which he belongs, the German attacks, the news he receives, the permissions, the climate and his fatigue, the attacks to retake Fleury and Thiaumont. Martial Daigueperse died around 1967. His notebooks were fully transcribed by his granddaughter.

title: AMSM-009_1 : Le journal de route de mon grand-père | The Journal of Onesime Maupiller
classification_reason: Scores are close, so this segment should be treated as a manual-review candidate.
text: I still have this little diary from my grandfather. He had given it to my father and it is from him that I now have it. My grandfather was called Onesime MAUPILLER and was a farmer in Saint Sauveur before the war. Moreover, he will become so again on his return from the war, in the same commune. He was incorporated on August 3, 1914 as a driver in the 49th artillery regiment, 1st colonial corps of Poitiers, 33rd section of colonial MI. I know very little about my grandfather and what his life was like in the war because he lived far from us. The distance, the character of my grandfather and my young age were not in favor of a discussion between him and me on this episode of his life. All that remains for me to relate to his story is this notebook, of which I cannot explain why it stops after the period 1914-1915, when I know that he remained at the front until 1918. .I think he wrote this notebook over the course of the war. It reads at the beginning "Recollection of the 1914 campaign, as if he thought the war would only last a year. Then the year 1915 was added. I think then he stopped keeping his diary. Among the things that I noted, there is this short passage in which he writes of having found a German in the forest, hanged with his belt.

title: AMSM-017_1 : Mon grand-père, ce hussard. | Barthélémy Rey's Picture And Letters From The Front
classification_reason: Scores are close, so this segment should be treated as a manual-review candidate.
text: My grandfather, Barthélémy REY, was born on March 13, 1881 in Saint Félix in Haute-Garonne. He was a winegrower before being mobilized in 1914. When his house was sold, we found a set of documents belonging to him relating to his participation in the conflict. It was in a way a discovery for me, because at home we finally talked about him very little and even less about the war, I just know that he would have been exposed to gas but I don't know under what conditions or in what proportions. He was very attached to his vineyard, and this can be seen in particular in his correspondence with his parents. He speaks just as much about the vine as about the war and his family when she writes to him, also speaks about the vine. Among his functions, I know that my grandfather was a trumpet. And what is surprising is that one of his cousins was too, as we can read in one of his letters. All these documents, I found them at the sale of my grandfather's house. I felt quite a strong emotion when I discovered their content, this image of a daily life which has not ceased despite the war.

His grandson never knew, reduced to imagining that he could have been mutilated during the First World War. But neither the 4 postcards that he kept, nor the consultation, in the Departmental Archives of Bas-Rhin (classification mark 844 D 164), of Victor Heymes' individual Veteran's file, provide an answer on this subject. .. Private, mobilized from August 2, 1914 to November 11, 1919 (sic), Victor Heymes was admitted to the benefit of the combatant's retirement in April 1940, after having asserted his participation in the fighting between the Narjowka and Zlota-Lipa rivers (Ukraine), from November 6 to 14, 1916 (then belonging to 5. Komp. Res. Infanterie Regt 5), in front of Jakobstadt and Kreuzburg (Latvia (?)), from July 2 to November 9, 1917, and in front of Reims, from 15 November to December 1, 1917 (then belonging to 3. Komp. Infanterie Regt 400). The two dated postcards that have come down to us, addressed by him on 07/24/1916 to his wife Sophie, and received by him from an acquaintance writing from Kilstett (Bas-Rhin) on 04/26/1918, tell us nothing more, except that he was stationed on this last date, with the 5. Komp. 1 Ers. Batl. Infantry Reg. 161, in Düren (North Rhine-Westphalia). Snippets of a journey that would require, to be better understood, additional investigations...

title: FRAN-PI-38 Histoire du volontaire Marcel Jean Eugène Migot | Marcel Migot' story
classification_reason: Scores are close, so this segment should be treated as a manual-review candidate.
text: House painter, Marcel Jean Eugène Migot is the son of Victor Edouard Migot and Clémence Anne Fougerol. Married to Germaine Berthe Filon, he has a daughter, Nicole Migot. Called on April 12, 1915 in Vannes (Meucon camp), he volunteered to join the front with the 116th infantry regiment, within the 29th Company. He was hospitalized twice: the first time, in Toulouse, from September 16 to December 20, 1916, following a concussion, and the second time, in Chateaudun, for a wound made in the thorax, when he fought at the Chemin des Ladies. Although he was seriously injured, he nevertheless returned to the front in 1918, once his convalescence was complete. His courage is rewarded with numerous decorations: Legion of Honor, military medal, war cross with palms (2 citations), combatant's cross, Verdun medal, Saint-Mihiel medal, medal of the Survivors of Aisne, medal of the wounded, silver and bronze medal of the voluntary combatants. After his return from the front, he wrote a lot about the war. Some of his texts are kept in the municipal archives of Saint-Denis. Others have been published, in particular Le Ravin de Veaudoine (in: Journal of the voluntary combatants). He also participated in the collective work Verdun: history of the fights which were fought from 1914 to 1918, on both banks of the Meuse, directed by Jacques Péricard and published in 1933.

title: AMSM-019_2 : Marius et Léon JAYNE | Marius and Léon Jayne at the Front
classification_reason: Scores are close, so this segment should be treated as a manual-review candidate.
text: Marius and Léon JAYNE are the two brothers of my grandmother Caroline JAYNE. Both died for France at the front. I have no memories because my aunts Louise and Joséphine were too upset to talk about it. On the site "Memory of Men", I present to you Marius and Léon JAYNE. Marius Julien Séraphin JAYNE born on January 27, 1891 in Ollioules, was a sergeant in the 1st company, 173rd Infantry Regiment, died for France on April 24, 1915 in Bois Buchot (Meuse) killed by the enemy. His brother Léon Jean JAYNE born on August 21, 1892 in Ollioules, was 2nd class in the 3rd Infantry Regiment, died for France on September 20, 1914 in Béthincourt (Meuse) missing in action. I had a photo of my two great-uncles during their class in Bonifacio (Corsica) then also a correspondence between the parents and their son, unfortunately when reading these letters, Marius died 3 days after his letter and Léon died 8 days later his last letter. After the return of a letter to one of their sons, my desperate great-grandparents speak in German to the enemy General Staff to have news of their sons at the front. The disappearance of my great-uncles was still a shock by these sad facts, which is why we didn't talk about it in my maternal family. See the story of Pierre IMBERT, Louise JAYNE

title: Ledermappe mit Fotos und Dokumenten von Wilhelm Große Munkenbeck | Leathercase with photos and documents of Wilhelm Große Munkenbeck
classification_reason: Scores are close, so this segment should be treated as a manual-review candidate.
text: Wilhelm (Willy) Große Munkenbeck from Bochum-Stiepel (August 23, 1890 - April 23, 1968) probably studied economics in London before the war. It is not known whether he volunteered for military service or was drafted. He was stationed as vice sergeant of the Bavarian Foot Artillery Battery No. 281 in the Lille/France area and later as a pilot in the 1st Royal Bavarian Flieger-Ersatzabteilung 1 in Schweissheim/Bavaria, where he probably stayed until 1918, as did his pilot's license , issued on February 16, 1918. His tasks included above all reconnaissance flights, on which he made numerous aerial photographs of militarily important areas on the western front, especially in France. Wilhelm regularly wrote postcards to his family in Bochum, and at one point during the war, as the family history relates, he found an opportunity to visit his parents in the Ruhr area. During a plane transfer, he threw the following message over the family home in Stiepel: "I'll be having coffee soon" and landed his plane in a nearby stubble field. After the coffee break, the plane was brought back into position with the help of a team of horses and Wilhelm Große Munkenbeck flew back to Munich. During one of his missions, he was wounded and sustained a bullet wound in his leg, but otherwise survived the war physically unharmed. The Grosse Munkenbeck family worked in agriculture. From 1951 until it was closed down in 1965, Wilhelm Große Munkenbeck operated the small colliery "Mit Gott dared" in Bochum. The bundle includes a collection of weapons, numerous photos, medals and uniform parts as well as other documents.

title: FRAD085-014 Journal de guerre d'Alfred Bonnaud | Alfred Bonnaud's diary
classification_reason: Scores are close, so this segment should be treated as a manual-review candidate.
text: When General Mobilization was decreed on August 2, 1914, Chief Warrant Officer Alfred Bonnaud had been serving for 17 years in the 35th Field Artillery Regiment based in Vannes (Morbihan). The first engagement of his battery took place in Maissin, Belgium, on August 22. For almost four years, Alfred took part in all phases of the war. On November 3, 1914, he changed assignments within the 1st battery. He takes command of the echelon, ie he leaves the firing battery to join the group in charge of battery support and logistics. On August 4, 1915, the 35th RAC was relieved, and Alfred went on leave for the first time. Direction Vannes, then Vix (Vendée). From November 1, 1917, Alfred was recalled to the gun battery to perform the duties of gunnery officer, then battery commander. Directed to the interior on April 11, 1918, it did not take part in the last battles. Admitted to retirement in 1920, he retired to Vix (Vendée). See the biographical note of Charles Julien Alfred Bonnaud in the Dictionary of Vendeans

title: AMSM-027_1 : Documents de mémoire | Nazaire François Prat's Medical Records from WWI
classification_reason: Scores are close, so this segment should be treated as a manual-review candidate.
text: My maternal grandfather Nazaire François Prat born on 8-07-1874, from the recruitment class of 1894, had been wounded in the foot on 9/06/1918, and gassed in the war. He went to different hospitals, Angers on 06/11/1918, Toulouse on 06/21/1918, at the Roanne reform center on 12/4/1918. He lived in Six-Fours where he died in 1934 at the age of 60. Intoxicated by gas, with emphysematous type breathing and sclerosis, the doctor gave the right to a pension following the infirmity. My mother didn't tell me about it. I looked for these documents, my mother couldn't throw them away! Through this contribution, the soldiers are not forgotten and the documents bear witness to a painful aftermath of the 1914-1918 war.

title: FRAD078-05 Le décès du capitaine Edmond Rousseau | Death of Edmond Rousseau
classification_reason: Scores are close, so this segment should be treated as a manual-review candidate.
text: The contributor is the grandson of Captain Edmond Rousseau. His story was told to him by his mother. The author of the letters is the captain himself, who died during the war. The photo was taken around 1902. " Edmond Rousseau was Captain in the 34th Colonial Infantry Regiment (based in Toulon), born April 17, 1872 in Boulogne-sur-Mer, he married January 23, 1902, to Madeleine Huret. In 1914 he had two daughters, Gabrielle 12 and Suzanne 8. He was mobilized from the first day, and left on Sunday August 2, 1914. In his hasty departure, he forgot his kepi (which he file), but urgently asks that the photos of Madeleine, Gaby and Suzon be sent to him in Toulon, whom he is very irritated at not having taken with him. In Paris, Aunt Marie Piriou, a cousin of Madeleine, l accompanies him to the Gare de Lyon, and stays with him until the train leaves at 2 p.m.

title: FRAD067-102 Albert Daltroff, Mosellan engagé dans l'armée allemande, correspond avec sa fiancée | Documents from Jean Daltroff
classification_reason: Scores are close, so this segment should be treated as a manual-review candidate.
text: Albert Daltroff, Mosellan, born in Montigny-les-Metz on July 22, 1885, was engaged in the German army in 1914, as a private. He corresponded between 1915 and 1918 with Léa Jacob, a resident of Vantoux (Wanten bei Metz) - with whom he became engaged just before the war and who would become his wife after the war. All the sendings from Albert Daltroff to his fiancée represent some 240 postcards. The views and dates of the few maps presented make it possible to globally reconstruct his journey: in July 1915, he was at Wesel, the assembly point for the German troops for the Western Front; in April 1916, he probably wrote from around Lille; sent to the eastern front, he found himself in Stettin (now Poland) in July 1916 and June 1917; in April 1918 he lived in Turek (now Poland). A translation project currently being developed for this correspondence, which his historian grandson, Jean Daltroff, plans to study, will provide details of this journey.

title: FRAD067-167 Franz Xavier Host, Alsacien engagé dans l'armée allemande, correspond avec les siens | Documents from Gérard Host
classification_reason: Scores are close, so this segment should be treated as a manual-review candidate.
text: Franz Xavier Host, Alsatian born December 2, 1885 is engaged in the field artillery of the German army from August 9, 1914 to November 25, 1918 as a private 2nd class. He gave news of the front to his relatives by regularly sending postcards and photographs addressed to his wife Amalia, his children and his father Joseph and recorded some personal memories of the period 1915-1917. His career can be reconstructed with the help of his "Militärdienstzeit Bescheinigung", issued on October 18, 1935 by the Reischsarchivzweigstelle of Stuttgart and appearing in his file as a veteran of the ONAC (transferred to the Departmental Archives of Bas-Rhin, 844 D 177): mobilized from August 3, 1914, he had several successive assignments: first assigned to the 15th army corps, in the infantry, in an ammunition column, he was sent, on August 31, 1915, to the Lazarett because of "Kniegelenksentzündung". He was sent on November 10, 1915 to the 3. Ers. beat Felda. Rgt n°80; from March 31, 1916, he was assigned to the 3. Ers. beat Felda. Rgt n°51, then from April 3, 1916, in the L. Munitions Kolonne II. Abtg. Felda. Rgt n°51. On April 28, 1916, he was sent to the Lazarett "mit Gelenkrheumatismus"; on June 21, 1916, he was sent back to the 3. Ers. beat Felda. Rgt n°51. Finally, from July 22, 1917 until November 23, 1918, the date of his demobilization, he was assigned to Flakbattr. No. 518 (anti-aircraft battery), in the artillery. His participation in several campaigns or his presence for more than two months in war zones are also reported: from October 30 to November 24, 1914, he took part in the battle near Ypres; from January 1 to August 31, 1915, to positional battles on the Yser; from April 3 to April 24, 1916, at the Battle of Verdun; from July 23 to December 31, 1917, to the battle and fighting in Flanders; from January 1 to November 11, 1918, the fighting in Flanders, the battle for Kemmel Hill, the fighting near Ypres, the fighting and the battle in Flanders. The second class Iron Cross was awarded to him on November 14, 1917. According to his correspondence, in January 1915 he was in Kortrijk (Belgium), in February–June 1915 in Wevelgem (Belgium); his cards were posted from Courtrai in August-September 1915, from Ghent at the end of September 1915; he wrote from Brussels in October 1915, then found himself in Neuf-brisach (Neubreisach) in Alsace from December 1915 to February 1916. In May-June 1916, he stayed in Bad Nauheim for medical reasons; he found himself in Ostend in November 1916, from July to December 1917 in Staden and from October 1917 to April 1918 in Beverne. He passed through Berlin in April 1918 and through Brussels, Louvain and Lichtervelde in September 1918. As a grandfather, he shared his memories of the war with his grandson, Gérard Host, and gradually gave him his documents and his belongings at each visit. of war.

title: FRAM - Journal de guerre de Jean Carré | Document from Jean Corentin Carré
classification_reason: Scores are close, so this segment should be treated as a manual-review candidate.
text: Jean Corentin Carré, born in Faouët (Morbihan), on January 9, 1900. On April 27, 1915, he enlisted at the age of 15 in the 41st infantry regiment. He arrived at the front at 15 years and 6 months and was assigned to the 410 infantry regiment. Section leader at 17, he earned the ranks of corporal, sergeant and adjutant, citations to the division, the army corps and the army. He died in aerial combat at the age of 18 and 3 months, on March 18, 1918. He was the youngest death for France in Faouët. In order to be able to engage, he invents an identity. His name was then Auguste Duthoy, born in Rumigny (Ardennes), on April 10, 1897. On December 29, 1916, from the depths of the trenches, he wrote a letter to Colonel Treillard, commander of the 410th infantry regiment. He asks to resume his identity, without leaving the front. This diary is kept at the Jean Carré college in Faouët, inaugurated in 1978.

title: FRAM - Le retour du corps de Charles Hamelin | Documents from Charles Hamelin
classification_reason: Scores are close, so this segment should be treated as a manual-review candidate.
text: Originally from Breteuil (Eure), Charles Hamelin joined the 9th Company of the 28th Infantry Regiment based in Evreux. In August 1918, Mrs. Hamelin was worried that she had no more news from her husband. At the end of the month, she was informed by a letter from her combat comrade, Private Jégou, that Charles had been killed by shrapnel on August 10. Jégou buried him near the village of Ressons, in the Oise. Her body was transferred to the Ressons military cemetery on July 18, 1919. Mrs. Hamelin then asked the Ministry of Pensions for the restitution of her husband's remains, which were to be exhumed on February 1, 1921. His remains, as well as those of seventy-five other soldiers from the Department of Eure who fell in combat, was repatriated by train to Evreux station, before being buried again in the Breteuil cemetery. Charles Hamelin obtained the Croix de Guerre posthumously on August 13, 1922.

title: AMSM-020_3 : Antoine-Marc Giorgetti | Antoine-Marc Giorgetti on board the ''Diane''
classification_reason: Scores are close, so this segment should be treated as a manual-review candidate.
text: Antoine-Marc Giorgetti a great-uncle born May 6, 1868 in Olcani in Corsica. I was fascinated by the numerous navigations carried out by him since 1888 and by other members of my family. Chief engineer in the navy, mobilized on a transport ship, the steam freighter ''Diane'' of the company of the United Charterers. The transport ship was attacked by a German submarine on June 6, 1917 in the Mediterranean off the coast of Oran. After firing for two hours, the ship began to sink, the crew put two canoes in the sea. After the torpedoing, the cargo ship was robbed of its supplies and tools by the Germans. Courageous attitude of the crew during the attack.

title: Omer Martin, soldat Maître-ouvrier du Génie, 1914-1919 | Omer Martin, Soldier Master Engineer, 1914-1919
classification_reason: Scores are close, so this segment should be treated as a manual-review candidate.
text: Farmer in Bailleau-sous-Gallardon (Eure-et-loir), Omer Martin was assigned to the 1st Engineer Regiment in Versailles in July 1914. His correspondence established his presence in the 1st line for two weeks in August 1915, then his stay in Etavigny (Oise) in December 1915. He was also present on the Craonne plateau, probably for the preparations for the Nivelle offensive of February 1917. 15 April 1917 for having "particularly distinguished himself during the period of preparation for the offensive and [having] valiantly participated in the pursuit of the enemy". Gassed and then suffering from gallstones, he stayed at the Bayonne military hospital between October and November 1918. Back in Versailles in January 1919, he was transferred in July to a placement center for demobilized soldiers.

title: FRAD062_047 - Léonide Thieullez, 1914. | Documents from Léonide Thieullez, 1914
classification_reason: Scores are close, so this segment should be treated as a manual-review candidate.
text: Born in August 1891 in Saint-Hilaire-les-Cambrai, Léonide Thieullez was a corporal in the 1st infantry regiment. He was reported missing on December 31, 1914 in La Harazée (Marne). His death was fixed on this date by judgment of the Civil Court of Cambrai dated January 28, 1921.

title: FRAM - Histoire de Jean Adolphe et Zoé GUIGUET-DORON | Documents from Jean-Adolphe et Zoé GUIGUET-DORON
classification_reason: Scores are close, so this segment should be treated as a manual-review candidate.
text: Jean Adolphe Guiguet-Doron was born on May 2, 1879 in Hauteluce, he is the son of Josephte Aimé Guiguet-Doron. Her sister Zoé Favre-Coutillet, born Guiguet-Doron, is the contributor's grandmother. Jean Adolphe's photograph and medals hung in his sister's room. The only element known to the contributor is the death of this great-uncle, who died for France in 1915; his memory was never mentioned in front of her. His identification sheet allows us to reconstruct his career: in 1899, he lived in Grenoble where he was a chimney sweep. He volunteered several times from 1897 in the 140th infantry regiment and then in the foreign legion. He was recalled at the time of general mobilization and incorporated into the 110th infantry regiment. He died on April 5, 1915, killed by the enemy at Flirey (Meurthe-et-Moselle). He posthumously received the Croix de Guerre with Palm and the Military Medal. His sister Zoé will keep her medals with her portrait and a letter from Private Lacroix dated November 27, 1915 giving her the list of effects found on her brother's body. The contributor found these objects among her father's belongings.

text: Enlisted on July 24, 1914 during his military service as an aide-de-camp assigned to the care of horses in Belgium, then a stretcher bearer in the 32nd Dragoons regiment until the end of the war, my maternal grandfather Fernand Greteau was quoted at the order of his regiment in September 1914 and August 1918 for his courage and bravery. title: FRAD008_6 Carnet de trousse d'un médecin durant la guerre | A doctor's diary
classification_reason: Scores are close, so this segment should be treated as a manual-review candidate.
text: Mr. Van Dijen, as part of his professional activity, discovered this "kit book" during the demolition of a middle-class house in Gouvray-sur-Marne (Seine-Saint-Denis) in the 1990s. It is the diary of a Parisian doctor, whose name is unknown, who was mobilized at the start of the war and confined to the Epinal field hospital (Vosges). From July to December 1914, he recounts day by day, the course of his day (his dreams, the weather, his meals, his guards ...), the most significant international events and his daily life as a war doctor seeing an influx of more and more injuries. He counts them and on September 13, 1914, already counts "46,259 wounded, sick, crippled seen and supplied at Épinal station until this day..."
